# Why Classical Architecture Loops Strain {#sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales}

::: {.epigraph}
> *"Feedback is the control of a system by reinserting into the system the results of its performance. If these results are merely used as numerical data for the criticism of the system and its regulation, we have the simple feedback of the control engineers."*
>
> — Norbert Wiener, *Cybernetics* (1948)
:::

::: {.column-margin}
**Author's Note:** Norbert Wiener, who founded cybernetics, made feedback the defining property of control. This chapter asks where a modern architecture loop loses that property when its state, choices, measurements, dispositions, or decisions no longer keep pace.
:::

```{=latex}
\abstract*{Architecture 2.0 is motivated by pressure inside computer architecture, not by enthusiasm for AI. The classical architecture loop remains valuable, but it strains as specialization, heterogeneous integration, memory movement, physical constraints, software velocity, and verification burden converge. Before adding AI, an architect must determine which part of that loop cannot keep up: representation, action, feedback, rejection, or commitment. Generative methods can intensify the pressure when they increase proposal volume without relieving the bound that already limits credible progress.}
```

::: {.callout-crux}
Before adding AI, which part of the existing design loop cannot keep up:
representation, action, feedback, rejection, or commitment?
:::

The loop in that question is the design loop, the working cycle every
architecture project already runs. It proposes a candidate, evaluates it against
workloads and tools, rejects or revises it on the evidence, and commits only
what the evidence supports. @sec-moonshot named Architecture 2.0, made the
design loop a first-class concern within that discipline, and made the claim
concrete with a compact lighthouse prompt. It asks for a low-power, RISC-V-based compute
subsystem for real-time mobile XR under a 3\ W, 3\ nm-class mobile envelope,
returned as a design-space report with evidence and rejected alternatives. The prompt is intentionally
small. The architecture state it implies is not.

This chapter explains why that pressure cannot be relieved by merely asking a
larger model to produce a larger answer. Computer architects already use
models, simulators, benchmarks, profilers, spreadsheets, compilers, register-transfer level (RTL) flows,
electronic design automation (EDA) tools, and expert reviews. The problem is not the absence of tools. The
problem is that the design loop that coordinates those tools is under pressure.
The state to represent, choices to search, feedback to obtain, candidates to
dispose of, and decisions to justify can grow faster than the loop that handles
them.

The claim is not that the old loop is obsolete. It is that the design loop must
become an inspectable execution and analysis mechanism within the bounded
architecture study it serves.

> **Scissors gap.** The scissors gap is the widening gap between the rate at
> which design choices, constraints, and evidence demands expand and the rate at
> which a team can obtain trusted feedback, reject weak candidates, and commit
> responsibly.

The gap may open at different points. The loop may omit state that later proves
decisive, expose the wrong actions, wait too long for measurements, accumulate
undisposed candidates, or lack a rule for advancing a decision. Proposal volume
is especially dangerous when trusted rejection is already saturated, but that
is one diagnosis rather than an explanation for every strained loop.

::: {.callout-war-story title="Generation outran rejection"}
**The claim.** In AlphaChip, a reinforcement-learning method for macro placement, learned placement was reported to compress a months-long placement loop to hours, and generating strong-looking placements only got faster [@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement]. That is the half of the story everyone remembers.

**The gap.** What did not keep pace was trusted, independent rejection. A candidate that looked excellent on the method's fast proxy still had to be checked against physical post-route metrics such as timing and routed wirelength, and an independent reassessment that ran that check reported strong classical baselines (such as simulated annealing) that the result had not cleared in that setup [@ChengEtAl2023AssessmentRL]. The generation side sped up while the accountable rejection side did not, and the space between them is where the dispute lives.

**The lesson.** This is the rejection side of the scissors gap. Proposal arrival outpaced trusted disposition capacity, so faster generation could not settle the disputed claim.
:::

The practical diagnostic is not "where can we add a model?" It is
"which part of the loop cannot keep up?" A loop may be representation-bound,
action-bound, feedback-bound, rejection-bound, or commitment-bound. Each names a
different part of the loop that saturates first, and each shows a different
symptom, needs a different record to confirm, and calls for a different first fix
(@tbl-loop-bottleneck-diagnostic).

| **Bound** | **Symptom** | **Lighthouse example** | **Record to inspect** | **First fix** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Representation-bound | The loop cannot encode the state that decides the outcome, so later evidence reverses its ranking. | It ranks XR compute organizations without representing memory-movement cost, so a later estimate overturns the proxy order. | Inputs, assumptions, workload state, interfaces, and omitted variables. | Add or refresh the state needed to explain the reversal. |
| Action-bound | The available choices are too narrow, too broad, or insufficiently constrained to reach a valid candidate. | The loop can retune parameters but cannot change the accelerator interface, so it never reaches the organization that would meet both the real-time deadline and the power envelope. | Choices the loop can actually make, invalid moves, and fixed interfaces. | Revise the allowed choices or the boundary of the task. |
| Feedback-bound | Relevant measurements are too scarce, slow, or expensive to keep up with the decisions that need them. | Only a few simulation-stage estimates are affordable, so most candidates remain weakly judged. | Measurement queues, latency, cost, coverage, and known proxy errors. | Reduce, redirect, or increase measurement capacity where it changes decisions. |
| Rejection-bound | Comparable proposals arrive faster than trusted dispositions can settle them, so the queue grows and weak candidates spill into expensive stages. | Lacking an early check, an accelerator that leads a cheap proxy ranking is not rejected until an expensive run exposes its data-movement cost. | Arrival rate, disposition rate, queue growth, and reasons for acceptance or rejection. | Control admission or raise trusted disposition capacity. |
| Commitment-bound | The process cannot say what the current evidence permits, so it over- or under-commits. | A candidate clears available checks, but nothing states whether that authorizes more exploration or an implementation decision. | Decision records, cited evidence, rollback cost, and accountable owner. | Limit the decision to what the current evidence supports. |

: **A diagnostic for which part of the loop cannot keep up:** Read across a row for one bound's symptom, its instance in the lighthouse prompt, the record that confirms the diagnosis, and the first fix to try. {#tbl-loop-bottleneck-diagnostic tbl-colwidths="[15,25,26,16,18]"}

The bounds are diagnostic categories, not five independent stages. One pressure
can expose several at once, and repairing one may move the bottleneck elsewhere.
The point here is to locate the first part that cannot keep up. @sec-architecture-20-ontology
then turns that diagnosis into a minimum review object for an architectural
claim.

The remainder of the chapter is therefore not a general technology-trend
survey. Each section supplies evidence for the diagnostic. Cadence exposes
commitment pressure. Architecture levers expose missing state. Specialization
and chiplets expand the action space and pull the software path inside it.
Software drift changes the workload that
state must describe. Physical constraints and engineering cost make feedback
scarce. Cheap proposal generation can then turn that scarcity into a rejection
queue.

Together, those pressures explain why the loop itself, not just the artifact,
becomes an object of design. They also explain why adding AI before locating the
bound can make the loop busier without making it more capable.

::: {.callout-learning-objectives}
After this chapter you can use the scissors gap to locate pressure in an
architecture loop before deciding where AI belongs. That means you can:

- **Recognize** the scissors gap between choices and trusted feedback in a real architecture setting;
- **Diagnose** whether a loop is limited by representation, action, feedback, rejection, or commitment;
- **Connect** cadence, search scale, workload drift, physical constraints, and engineering cost to the bound they stress;
- **Identify** where generic AI assumptions break at architecture boundaries;
- **Decide** whether AI would relieve the diagnosed bound or merely move pressure elsewhere.
:::

## Classical Loops Already Use Feedback

The design loop did not arrive out of nowhere. Computer architecture has always
worked through feedback and revision. A typical loop begins with an
intent: improve latency, reduce energy, raise throughput, support a workload,
or fit a system into a power and cost envelope. The architect then chooses an
abstraction, builds or selects a model, runs an analysis or simulation, studies
the result, revises the design, and repeats. Eventually the work crosses into
implementation, validation, verification, and signoff. That basic pattern is
visible in textbook architecture practice and in industrial design loops
[@HennessyPatterson2017QuantitativeApproach].

A traditional SPEC CPU-style study makes the loop concrete. An architect might
choose a subset of SPEC CPU workloads, propose a cache hierarchy or branch
predictor change, run a simulator or performance model, inspect IPC (instructions per cycle), miss
rates, branch-misprediction rates, area and power proxies, reject candidates
that help one workload while hurting others, and repeat. Human judgment enters
repeatedly in tasks like selecting workloads, deciding which proxy is credible, noticing
unexpected behavior, choosing when a candidate is worth deeper analysis, and
deciding which risk to accept. SPEC CPU 2017, for example, was designed as an
industry-standardized suite for compute-intensive performance, stressing
processor, memory subsystem, and compiler behavior
[@SPEC2017CPU]. The important point is not the specific suite version. It is
the loop discipline, which requires a bounded workload set, an explicit model, comparable
metrics, rejection of weak candidates, and expert judgment about whether the
evidence is strong enough.

@sec-moonshot made the Architecture 1.0 to Architecture 2.0 loop shift visible in
@fig-design-loop-breaks. The point here is why that shift becomes necessary.
Classical architecture loops already have intent, models, candidates, tool runs,
and expert review. They strain when the state, action boundaries, evidence,
rejection, and decision authority become too large to remain mostly implicit.

For the lighthouse prompt, the difference is not that Architecture 2.0 invents
review discipline. The difference is what the loop must record before a result
can be trusted (@tbl-classical-vs-arch2-review).

| **Classical review might record** | **Architecture 2.0 additionally requires** |
| --- | --- |
| Candidate design and measured result. | Enough workload, assumption, and interface state to explain what was evaluated. |
| Tool output or benchmark score. | Enough tool context, cost, coverage, and known limitations to judge the feedback. |
| Expert judgment about whether the result looks plausible. | The accepted and rejected alternatives, decision scope, and accountable owner. |

: **Architecture 2.0 makes review pressure inspectable:** Disciplined architects already review results. The additional record shows whether missing state, tool limits, unresolved alternatives, or decision ambiguity is straining that review. {#tbl-classical-vs-arch2-review tbl-colwidths="[40,50]"}

None of the added record replaces classical review; each row pairs something
reviewers already trust (a result, a score, an expert's read) with the state,
tool context, and ownership that make that trust checkable. The classical loop
is powerful because it does not require perfect automation. It
combines formal models, approximations, domain knowledge, and review. It also
rests on an unstated balance: the state, number of choices, cost of evaluation, and
the evidence needed to make progress must remain within the capacity of the
team and tools. When that balance holds, the loop works. When it breaks, one of
the five bounds becomes visible. The five-part diagnostic shows whether the
failure lies in representation, action, feedback, rejection, or
accountable commitment.

## Cadence and Gates Manage Risk

Commitment-bound pressure appears when a team cannot say how far a decision may
advance or how much change the next step can absorb. Industrial practice has
long managed that pressure through cadence. Intel's
tick-tock model is the cleanest familiar example. A "tick" moved a
known microarchitecture to a new process technology; a "tock" introduced a new
microarchitecture on a more mature process. The point was not that product
development was literally two steps. The point was risk isolation. Avoid
changing every hard thing at once, preserve a cadence, and let evidence from
one step inform the next. When node transitions lengthened, Intel described a
move toward process-architecture-optimization, explicitly using longer-lived
14\ nm and 10\ nm process technologies while further optimizing products and
processes to maintain product cadence [@Intel2015Form10K].

That history is useful for Architecture 2.0 because it shows that a design loop
is not only a sequence of tools. It is also a policy for what may change, what
evidence permits the next step, and how the organization responds when the cost
or latency of feedback changes. A cadence change is therefore a commitment
diagnosis. The old allocation of risk no longer fits the loop.

This is not the first time architecture has had to redesign its loop. The rows
in @tbl-historical-loop-transformations show how earlier shifts made an
interface, rule, workload, tool path, or decision criterion explicit. Read them
as continuity evidence rather than as a history survey. The field repeatedly
made hard problems tractable by exposing the part of the process that could no
longer remain implicit.

| **Shift** | **What the loop made explicit** | **Architecture 2.0 lesson** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| System/360 compatibility | A stable Instruction Set Architecture (ISA) contract separated architecture from implementation across a product family [@AmdahlBlaauwBrooks1964System360]. | Architecture is a durable interface and commitment policy, not only a circuit or microarchitecture. |
| Mead--Conway VLSI and MOSIS | Design rules, layout abstractions, and fabrication access turned custom-chip design into a shareable and reusable loop [@MeadConway1980VLSI; @USCISI2025MOSISFirstYear]. | Representation and access to feedback can change who can participate in architecture work. |
| RISC | Workload, compiler, Very Large-Scale Integration (VLSI), and the quantitative cost/performance case became the explicit architectural argument [@PattersonDitzel1980RISC]. | Evidence can reject attractive complexity when the full loop cost is visible. |
| SPEC-style benchmarking | Workload selection, run rules, reporting conventions, and comparability became community infrastructure [@SPEC2017CPU]. | Benchmarks are loop governance, not just input programs. |
| Logic synthesis and timing closure | Hardware Description Language (HDL), libraries, constraints, and timing reports gave downstream tools authority to reject upstream choices [@DeMicheli1994SynthesisOptimization]. | Implementation feedback belongs in the architecture loop before commitment. |
| CUDA-style GPU programming | Kernels, thread hierarchies, memory spaces, libraries, and toolchains made specialized hardware programmable [@NickollsEtAl2008CUDA]. | Specialized hardware succeeds only when the software loop is designed with it. |

: **Architecture progress often comes from redesigning the loop:** Historical shifts became durable when they exposed a representation, interface, tool path, benchmark, or evidence gate that let the community coordinate work. {#tbl-historical-loop-transformations tbl-colwidths="[22,39,28]"}

These examples should make the Architecture 2.0 claim less exotic. The new
challenge is that AI methods can propose, search, summarize, and optimize at a
scale that may expose whichever part of the loop is already closest to its
limit.

## Architecture Levers Add State

Representation-bound pressure appears when the process no longer carries the
state needed to distinguish promising candidates from misleading ones. That
state accumulated as architecture advanced by adding levers. For decades, technology scaling made
the same basic design style better by providing smaller, faster, cheaper, and
more energy-efficient transistors. As the Dennard-era energy story weakened
(@sec-moonshot), the field leaned harder on microarchitecture, instruction-level
parallelism, caches, speculation, vector units, multicore, accelerators,
specialization, and system-level optimization. The result is not a simple
failure narrative. It is an accumulation of levers.

The accumulation matters because each lever creates both opportunity and
obligation. Better microarchitecture adds policies and corner cases. Multicore adds
coherence, synchronization, memory ordering, and workload partitioning.
Specialization improves efficiency when the workload and software stack are
understood, but it adds interfaces, data movement, programmability, and
verification burden. Chiplets and heterogeneous integration, the composition lever, promise
modularity and scaling beyond a monolithic die, but they add partitioning, die-to-die
interfaces, package-level constraints, test, yield, thermal coupling, and
supply-chain questions.

The field keeps adding levers because efficiency still matters
(@fig-architecture-levers). But the same moves that recover efficiency also
increase the burden of representing the design state and producing trusted
feedback.

![**Architecture levers add study state:** Scaling, microarchitecture, optimization, specialization, and composition create new opportunities for efficiency, but each also adds constraints, interfaces, tool feedback, rejected alternatives, and evidence that a credible comparison must carry.](images/F2-architecture-levers){#fig-architecture-levers width="100%" fig-alt="Horizontal chain of architecture levers from scaling through composition, showing that each efficiency lever adds study state and evidence obligations."}

While @fig-architecture-levers illustrates a broad accumulation of design levers, the post-Dennard turn to specialization (@sec-moonshot) explains why the newest levers cluster at the specialization and composition end of the chain, the moment Hennessy and Patterson framed as a new golden age for architecture [@HennessyPatterson2019GoldenAge]. Architecture 2.0 should be read in that
lineage, but with an explicit connection to the RISC revolution that preceded it. Levers only add usable state if the loop can compare candidates rigorously, and that comparison discipline is exactly what RISC established. RISC was not just a simplified instruction set; it was the realization that making an architectural claim required a compiler, a benchmark, and a simulator. Architecture 1.0 gave us the tools to measure IPC and execution time, anchoring the field to the Iron Law of Performance [@EmerClark1984VAXCharacterization] ($\text{Time} = \frac{\text{Instructions}}{\text{Program}} \times \frac{\text{Cycles}}{\text{Instruction}} \times \frac{\text{Time}}{\text{Cycle}}$).

Architecture 2.0 does not break the Iron Law. It extends the same quantitative
discipline to the process that compares candidates. The new golden age gives
the field more architectural levers, and each lever adds state that a credible
comparison may need. If the loop cannot carry that state, adding AI enlarges the
space without repairing the representation bound.

## Specialization and Chiplets Expand Search

Action-bound pressure appears when the process cannot express or constrain the
choices needed to reach a valid design. Specialization makes that pressure easy
to see because efficiency claims are multidimensional, the
point @sec-moonshot made explicit. The binding constraint differs across scales,
from a battery- and thermally-limited mobile part to a warehouse-scale system
bounded by power delivery and total cost of ownership (TCO)
[@BarrosoHolzleRanganathan2019DatacenterAsComputer]. What this chapter adds is
the consequence for the loop. Specialization does not just change which metric
matters; it multiplies the decisions the loop must evaluate.

Specialization increases the number of architectural decisions because the
architect must decide what to specialize, where to specialize it, and how it
communicates with the rest of the system. A low-power XR subsystem is not just
a choice between CPU and accelerator. It raises questions about vector length,
memory hierarchy, local buffers, compression, dataflow, quantization, runtime
scheduling, compiler support, sensor streams, display deadlines, thermal
behavior, and fallback modes.

Chiplets compound the effect. They make it possible to compose systems from
multiple dies and to mix process technologies, IP blocks, and memory
technologies. But a chiplet system is not simply a bigger board-level system
inside a package. The package changes latency, bandwidth, energy, thermal
coupling, test, repair, physical constraints, and business boundaries. Open
standards such as Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) aim to make die-to-die integration more composable by
standardizing interface layers, protocols, software models, and compliance
expectations [@UCIeConsortium2026Spec]. That standardization is valuable,
but it also makes the architecture question more explicit. What should be
partitioned, through which interface, under which evidence standard?

The combinatorics are easy to understate. The following arithmetic is
illustrative, not a measurement. Suppose a team is exploring only a narrow slice
of the lighthouse prompt, an accelerator and memory subsystem for one
workload family from XRBench [@KwonEtAl2023XRBench]. Even if it considers five compute
organizations, four vector or accelerator interface choices, six memory
hierarchy choices, four interconnect choices, three voltage/frequency
policies, three compiler/runtime policies, and three verification or fidelity
levels, the crude product is already:

```{python}
#| echo: false
toy_design_loop_states = 5 * 4 * 6 * 4 * 3 * 3 * 3
```

$$
5 \times 4 \times 6 \times 4 \times 3 \times 3 \times 3
$$
That is `{python} f"{toy_design_loop_states:,}"` candidate configurations before workload versions, process corners, thermal
constraints, and reliability cases are counted, and before any configuration
has been rejected. This
number is intentionally conservative. More realistic architecture-adjacent
loops quickly become much larger, or much slower, even before final silicon
evidence is involved.

The product is also not just a counting problem. If each surviving state needs
an analytical model, simulator run, synthesis check, or human review, a larger
action space also creates feedback pressure. Cheap models reduce cost, but they
do not determine whether the available choices are legal or whether later
measurements will preserve their ranking. The sequence from
@sec-data-representations-world-models through
@sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization develops those representation,
environment, and method questions after this chapter has located the pressure.

In the lighthouse slice, the same count is not search-space trivia. The
CPU/accelerator/System-on-Chip (SoC) choice fixes interfaces, memory paths,
compiler/runtime assumptions, physical constraints, and the set of candidates
that available tools can actually evaluate.

Beyond the lighthouse example, the same combinatorial pressure shows up in other concrete architecture
settings, grounded in @tbl-design-loop-scale-anchors. The examples are not meant to be a single measurement scale; they show
that mapping, design-space exploration (DSE), physical design, and software
tuning each impose a different
kind of loop burden. Timeloop, an analytical framework for evaluating and mapping
deep-neural-network (DNN) accelerators, makes the mapping case especially
concrete. For a single convolutional-neural-network (CNN) layer with seven nested
loop dimensions, mapped onto a four-level memory hierarchy, the number of legal
mappings is astronomically large. Before the raw expression, consider what is being multiplied. For every level of the memory hierarchy, we must permute the order of the seven loop dimensions, and for every tensor, we must decide whether it bypasses each memory level. The exact expression matters less than its
size. The unconstrained mapspace contains $(7!)^4 \times (2^4)^3$ arrangements
before co-factor choices (such as tiling sizes) are even counted. The $(7!)^4$ factor counts loop-order
permutations of the seven nested loop dimensions (e.g., batch, output channels, input channels, and spatial dimensions) at each of the four memory levels,
and $(2^4)^3$ counts the level-bypass choices that decide which of the three tensors (inputs, weights, outputs) skip
any of the four memory levels [@ParasharEtAl2019Timeloop].

| **Loop example** | **Scale anchor** | **Loop lesson** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| DNN accelerator mapping | Timeloop exposes loop permutations, factorization choices, and level-bypass alternatives. | Mapping is itself a combinatorial problem; architecture evaluation depends on the mapper and its constraints. |
| DNN accelerator DSE | MAESTRO, an analytical cost model for estimating DNN dataflow performance, reports 480M candidates, 2.5M valid designs, and 0.17M designs/s [@KwonEtAl2019MAESTRO]. | Validity and pruning shape what evidence can be trusted. |
| TPU-block floorplanning | Learned floorplanning reported a months-to-hours loop compression; later work challenged baselines and reproducibility [@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement; @ChengEtAl2023AssessmentRL]. | Search speed alone does not settle a comparison whose baselines and measurements remain disputed. |
| Tensor-program tuning | AutoTVM, a machine learning-based tensor program optimizer, describes tensor-operator search spaces on the order of billions of possible implementations for a single GPU operator [@ChenEtAl2018AutoTVM]. | The software side of specialization also has a large hardware-dependent loop. |

: **The scissors gap has source-backed scale anchors:** Mapping, accelerator DSE, floorplanning, and tensor-program tuning all show candidate scale, validity, feedback cost, and evidence standards growing together. {#tbl-design-loop-scale-anchors tbl-colwidths="[25,36,28]"}

The exact size of any one space is not the point. These examples differ in
task, fidelity, and tool chain, but they share the same pressure pattern.
Candidate count, invalid choices, and evaluation demand grow together.
MAESTRO's throughput also shows the trap from the other side. At 0.17M designs
per second, the full 480M-candidate space clears in under an hour, so cheap
analytical feedback is not the scarce resource. The scarce resource is the
trusted disposition that each cheap winner eventually demands. Placed on one
log axis against an affordable trusted-evaluation budget, only the
deliberately conservative toy loop even approaches what trusted disposition
can cover (@fig-search-vs-eval-gap).

```{python}
#| label: fig-search-vs-eval-gap
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **Search spaces outgrow affordable trusted evaluation:** The chapter's scale anchors on one log axis, from the toy SoC subsystem count computed above to Timeloop's unconstrained mapspace [@ParasharEtAl2019Timeloop; @KwonEtAl2019MAESTRO; @ChenEtAl2018AutoTVM], against a shaded band marking how many trusted dispositions one study can afford. Filled markers are stated or computed counts; the hollow marker is AutoTVM's order-of-magnitude estimate. The band is an illustrative assumption, not a measurement.
#| out-width: "92%"
#| fig-alt: "Log-scale strip chart plotting four search-space sizes as points: a toy SoC loop at about 13 thousand configurations, MAESTRO's 480 million candidates, AutoTVM's roughly one billion tensor programs, and Timeloop's 2.6 times ten to the eighteenth unconstrained mappings. A shaded green band from one hundred to one hundred thousand marks an illustrative affordable trusted-evaluation budget; only the toy loop falls inside it, and the Timeloop mapspace lies about thirteen orders of magnitude beyond it."

from math import factorial

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, add_note_box, apply_style, row_axis, top_log_axis

# Data provenance (all values transcribed or computed from this chapter's text):
# - Toy SoC subsystem loop: 5*4*6*4*3*3*3 = 12,960, computed earlier in this chapter.
# - MAESTRO accelerator DSE: 480M candidates, stated [@KwonEtAl2019MAESTRO].
# - AutoTVM tensor programs: "on the order of billions" per GPU operator, stated
#   qualitatively (order-of-magnitude, hollow marker) [@ChenEtAl2018AutoTVM].
# - Timeloop unconstrained mapspace: (7!)^4 * (2^4)^3 ~= 2.6e18, computed from the
#   stated expression [@ParasharEtAl2019Timeloop]. Values not recomputed from sources.
# - Trusted-evaluation band 1e2..1e5 dispositions per study: illustrative assumption
#   (minutes to hours per trusted disposition over a weeks-long study budget), not a
#   measurement. As-of: 2026-07-14.

toy_states = 5 * 4 * 6 * 4 * 3 * 3 * 3
timeloop_mapspace = factorial(7) ** 4 * (2**4) ** 3

rows = [
    {"label": "Toy SoC subsystem loop", "note": "computed in this chapter", "value": toy_states, "right": "$1.3\\times10^{4}$", "stated": True},
    {"label": "MAESTRO accelerator DSE", "note": "480M candidates, stated", "value": 4.8e8, "right": "$4.8\\times10^{8}$", "stated": True},
    {"label": "AutoTVM tensor programs", "note": "order of billions, approximate", "value": 1e9, "right": "$\\sim\\!10^{9}$", "stated": False},
    {"label": "Timeloop unconstrained mapspace", "note": "$(7!)^{4}\\times(2^{4})^{3}$, computed", "value": timeloop_mapspace, "right": "$2.6\\times10^{18}$", "stated": True},
]

band_low, band_high = 1e2, 1e5

apply_style()
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4.95, 2.75))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.42, right=0.82, top=0.79, bottom=0.22)

row_axis(ax, len(rows))
top_log_axis(
    ax,
    xlim=(2e1, 4e19),
    xticks=[1e2, 1e5, 1e8, 1e11, 1e14, 1e17],
    xticklabels=["$10^{2}$", "$10^{5}$", "$10^{8}$", "$10^{11}$", "$10^{14}$", "$10^{17}$"],
    xlabel="search-space size (candidate configurations)",
)

# Affordable trusted-evaluation band: canonical evidence tint (#E7F5EC from the
# shared tint set; no COLORS key exists for tints) paired with the evidence ink edge.
ax.axvspan(band_low, band_high, facecolor="#E7F5EC", zorder=0)
for edge in (band_low, band_high):
    ax.axvline(edge, color=COLORS["evidence_ink"], linewidth=0.8, linestyle=(0, (4, 3)), zorder=1)
ax.text(
    (band_low * band_high) ** 0.5,
    3.42,
    "affordable trusted-evaluation\nband (illustrative)",
    ha="center",
    va="center",
    fontsize=5.8,
    fontweight="bold",
    color=COLORS["evidence_ink"],
    linespacing=1.2,
    bbox={"facecolor": "white", "edgecolor": "none", "pad": 0.8, "alpha": 0.85},
    zorder=4,
)

for y, row in enumerate(rows):
    ax.axhline(y, color=COLORS["row"], linewidth=0.65, zorder=0)
    if row["stated"]:
        ax.scatter([row["value"]], [y], s=26, marker="o", facecolor=COLORS["designspace"], edgecolor=COLORS["designspace"], linewidth=1.0, zorder=3)
    else:
        ax.scatter([row["value"]], [y], s=26, marker="o", facecolors="white", edgecolors=COLORS["designspace"], linewidth=1.2, zorder=3)
    ax.text(-0.86, y - 0.17, row["label"], transform=ax.get_yaxis_transform(), ha="left", va="center", fontsize=6.6, fontweight="bold", color=COLORS["ink"], clip_on=False)
    ax.text(-0.86, y + 0.23, row["note"], transform=ax.get_yaxis_transform(), ha="left", va="center", fontsize=5.4, color=COLORS["muted"], clip_on=False)
    ax.text(1.04, y, row["right"], transform=ax.get_yaxis_transform(), ha="left", va="center", fontsize=6.1, fontweight="bold", color=COLORS["designspace_ink"], clip_on=False)

# Gap annotation on the widest anchor row: band edge to the Timeloop mapspace.
ax.annotate(
    "",
    xy=(timeloop_mapspace / 6, 3.0),
    xytext=(band_high, 3.0),
    arrowprops={"arrowstyle": "<->", "color": COLORS["muted"], "linewidth": 0.8},
)
ax.text(
    (band_high * timeloop_mapspace) ** 0.5,
    2.68,
    "$\\approx$13 orders of magnitude beyond the band",
    ha="center",
    va="center",
    fontsize=5.8,
    color=COLORS["muted"],
)

add_note_box(
    fig,
    "Band assumes $10^{2}$ to $10^{5}$ trusted dispositions per study (minutes to hours each over a weeks-long budget); an assumption, not a measured limit.",
    xywh=(0.12, 0.040, 0.76, 0.085),
    fontsize=5.4,
)

plt.show()
plt.close(fig)
```

Every other anchor sits between roughly four and thirteen orders of magnitude
beyond the band, so the diagnostic asks whether the action space itself is
wrong or whether the next bound, feedback, now limits progress.

## Specialized Hardware Needs a Software Loop

The action boundary is not solely a hardware boundary. Specialization also
exposes a software obligation. It is one thing to build an
accelerator, vector unit, memory hierarchy, or chiplet partition that looks
efficient in isolation. It is another thing to let programmers, compilers,
runtimes, libraries, and deployment systems use it without destroying the
efficiency claim through data movement, synchronization, code-generation
overhead, or maintenance burden.

The historical examples in @tbl-historical-loop-transformations show the same pattern.
RISC depended on a compiler story. CUDA, NVIDIA's parallel computing platform and programming model, made GPU specialization useful by
making the programming and toolchain loop explicit [@NickollsEtAl2008CUDA].
The tensor-program row in @tbl-design-loop-scale-anchors pushes the point further.
Billion-scale operator search is already a software-side obligation of
specialization. Systems such as Halide and MLIR, advanced compiler infrastructures used to define and schedule domain-specific operations, make scheduling, lowering, and
intermediate representations central parts of the performance loop
[@RaganKelleyEtAl2017HalideCACM; @LattnerEtAl2020MLIR].

::: {.callout-field-note title="The architecture bet the compiler never paid: Itanium"}
Itanium's EPIC (Explicitly Parallel Instruction Computing) architecture rested on one claim, that a compiler could
statically expose enough instruction-level parallelism to feed a very wide
machine [@SchlanskerRau2000EPIC]. The architecture's useful action space
therefore included compiler behavior, not only issue width. Compiler-achieved
instruction-level parallelism on representative workloads was a measurement of
whether that software obligation held.

Intel's Larrabee made a related software bet, that software
rasterization on many x86 cores would match fixed-function GPUs
[@SeilerEtAl2008Larrabee], and Transmeta's Crusoe bet that code-morphing
software would let a simple core match x86 [@DehnertEtAl2003Transmeta]. These
examples differ in outcome and context, but each makes the same diagnostic point.
Software capability was part of the architectural state and action boundary.

**Lesson:** A hardware proposal is action-bound when the software path needed to use it lies outside the choices the loop can inspect and evaluate.
:::

For the lighthouse prompt, this means that a "64-bit RISC-V compute subsystem"
cannot be judged by hardware structure alone. If the answer proposes a vector
extension, custom accelerator, memory-local dataflow, or chiplet boundary, the
loop must also represent how code reaches that mechanism, what compiler or
runtime assumptions are required, which libraries or kernels use it, and which
tests reject a design that is efficient only in a hand-written kernel. The
software path is not downstream polish. It is part of the architectural claim.

::: {.callout-lighthouse title="RISC-V is a software contract"}
**Context.** The ISA phrase fixes more than an instruction encoding. It names
the boundary where AI-generated hardware choices become visible to compilers, runtimes,
libraries, operating systems, and compatibility tests.

**In the lighthouse prompt.** "64-bit RISC-V-based" and "vector-capable CPU,
accelerator, or SoC block" make the software path part of the claim. An AI-assisted loop
must explain how code reaches the mechanism, what ABI, memory-model, and
toolchain assumptions it preserves, and what tests reject unsupported semantics.

**Takeaway.** The ISA is part of the evidence path for whether the AI-proposed
subsystem can actually be used, not a label on the hardware box.
:::

## Software Changes Faster Than Silicon {#sec-software-changes-faster-than-silicon}

Representation-bound pressure returns when the workload state becomes stale,
and commitment-bound pressure follows when a long-lived hardware decision rests
on that state. Modern software stacks move quickly. AI models change. Precision
formats (e.g., FP8, INT4, block-scale formats[^fn-hw-block-scale-formats]) and
sparsity patterns (the structured or unstructured zeros in weights or
activations that hardware can skip) continue to change, while hardware places
bets on data types years in advance. Compiler passes change. Kernel libraries
change. Runtimes, serving systems, quantization formats, batching strategies,
fleet policies, and benchmark versions change. The hardware design cycle does
not move at the same pace.

[^fn-hw-block-scale-formats]: Block-scale formats share a single exponent across a block of low-precision mantissas to maintain dynamic range with minimal memory footprint. They are one of the fast-drifting numeric targets a silicon program must commit its datapaths to years ahead, so betting hardware on them is exactly the workload-stability wager this section warns the loop to make only with evidence.

Generative AI can sharpen this mismatch rather than easing it. If models lower
the marginal cost of producing code, a Jevons-like effect[^fn-econ-jevons-paradox] can increase software
volume and churn rather than simply making the same workload cheaper. AI coding
assistants had been adopted at scale by the mid-2020s. The 2024 Stack Overflow Developer Survey reported that a
majority of professional developers were already using AI tools in their
workflow [@StackOverflow2024DeveloperSurvey].
The design target can therefore become the output of a faster software loop
rather than a workload an architect samples once. A silicon program may then be
committed against a snapshot the software has moved past. The
architect cannot answer this by demanding faster silicon alone, because the
fabrication cycle and physical closure set a floor on how short the hardware loop
can be.

[^fn-econ-jevons-paradox]: Jevons' paradox, stated by the economist W. S. Jevons in *The Coal Question* (1865), is the observation that making a resource cheaper to use can raise its total consumption instead of lowering it. Here it warns that cheaper AI-assisted code generation can swell software volume and churn rather than steadying the target, widening the workload drift the silicon loop must bet against and shortening the life of any specialization commitment.

This forces a familiar tradeoff into the diagnosis. Specialization can improve
efficiency while reducing tolerance for workload drift, whereas generality
preserves agility at a cost in performance per watt. The response is not to
abandon specialization. It is to bound the workload, measure its drift, and ask
whether the hardware decision is still supported by the workload state on which
it was based.

The drift runs in both directions, though. After a design ships, part of the
software loop bends toward it, as compilers, kernels, and runtimes mature
around the committed silicon and recover performance the launch evaluation
could not see. A lighthouse diagnosis should separate that convergent
enablement from the divergent churn this section describes, because one
quietly strengthens a commitment's support while the other erodes it
(@sec-loop-patterns-across-stack returns to this in the deployment-facing
loops).

The scale of the target workload is not static either. For the loop, the important fact
is not the compute-growth curve itself; it is that workload records expire. The
compute behind notable AI systems has grown by roughly twenty orders of
magnitude in nearly seven decades (@fig-ai-compute-growth), so the workloads an
architecture must serve can shift faster than a silicon program can absorb the
change. Frontier training compute rose more than tenfold within the most
recent 3-4 year design cadence alone. A
lighthouse diagnosis should compare the versions of the model, compiler,
runtime, benchmark, trace, and deployment assumptions used at each decision.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ai-compute-growth
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **AI compute demand is a fast-moving target:** Training compute for notable AI systems has grown by roughly twenty orders of magnitude since the Perceptron, with a sharp acceleration in the deep-learning era after 2012 and continued frontier growth well past GPT-4 [@SevillaEtAl2022ComputeTrends]. Points are notable-model training-compute estimates from Epoch AI's database; the dashed line is the deep-learning-era trend fit to measured points. Filled markers are database estimates; the hollow 2025 frontier marker is an order-of-magnitude approximation excluded from that fit. The shaded band marks a representative 3-4 year silicon design cadence; within one such window, frontier training compute grew by more than an order of magnitude. The architecture that serves these systems is designed against workloads whose scale and shape can change faster than a silicon program can respond.
#| out-width: "78%"
#| fig-alt: "Log-scale scatter of AI training compute by year for a dozen notable models, rising about twenty orders of magnitude from the 1958 Perceptron to frontier 2024-2025 systems, with a steep deep-learning-era trend line after 2012."

import math
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, apply_style

apply_style()

# (year, training-compute FLOP, label, show_label). Notable-model estimates
# from Epoch AI's database; the three-eras trend is from Sevilla et al. 2022.
# Mantissas are estimates; the 2025 frontier point is order-of-magnitude only.
points = [
    (1958, 7e5,    "Perceptron", True),
    (2012, 4.7e17, "AlexNet", True),
    (2014, 1.6e19, "VGG-16", False),
    (2016, 1.9e23, "AlphaGo", False),
    (2017, 7.4e18, "Transformer", False),
    (2018, 2.9e20, "BERT-Large", True),
    (2019, 1.5e21, "GPT-2", False),
    (2020, 3.1e23, "GPT-3", True),
    (2021, 6.3e23, "Gopher", False),
    (2022, 2.5e24, "PaLM", False),
    (2023, 2.1e25, "GPT-4", True),
    (2024, 3.8e25, "Llama 3.1 405B", False),
    (2025, 1e26,   "frontier (est.)", True),
]

pre = [p for p in points if p[0] < 2012]
dl = [p for p in points if p[0] >= 2012]
# Fit only measured points: the outlier RL run skews the slope, and the
# estimated frontier point must not steer the trend it is compared against.
fit = [p for p in dl if p[2] not in ("AlphaGo", "frontier (est.)")]

# plain log-linear least squares for the deep-learning-era trend line
fx = [p[0] for p in fit]
fy = [math.log10(p[1]) for p in fit]
n = len(fx)
mx = sum(fx) / n
my = sum(fy) / n
slope = sum((x - mx) * (y - my) for x, y in zip(fx, fy)) / sum((x - mx) ** 2 for x in fx)
intercept = my - slope * mx
tx = [2012, 2026]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4.65, 2.45))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.16, right=0.96, top=0.95, bottom=0.18)
ax.plot(tx, [10 ** (intercept + slope * x) for x in tx], color=COLORS["muted"],
        linewidth=1.0, linestyle=(0, (4, 3)), zorder=1)
ax.scatter([p[0] for p in pre], [p[1] for p in pre], s=20, color=COLORS["muted"], zorder=3)
meas = [p for p in dl if p[2] != "frontier (est.)"]
est = [p for p in dl if p[2] == "frontier (est.)"]
ax.scatter([p[0] for p in meas], [p[1] for p in meas], s=24, color=COLORS["blue"], zorder=3)
ax.scatter([p[0] for p in est], [p[1] for p in est], s=24, facecolors="white",
           edgecolors=COLORS["blue"], linewidths=1.1, zorder=3)
ax.set_yscale("log")
ax.set_ylim(1e4, 1e28)
ax.set_xlim(1953, 2028)
ax.set_ylabel("training compute (FLOP)", fontsize=7)
ax.set_xticks([1960, 1980, 2000, 2020])
ax.tick_params(axis="both", labelsize=6.5, length=2.5, width=0.6, pad=2)
# (dx pts, dy pts, ha): late-2010s labels sit left of their points so they
# stay inside the axes and clear of neighboring markers.
offsets = {
    "Perceptron": (8, -3, "left"),
    "AlexNet": (7, -8, "left"),
    "BERT-Large": (7, -3, "left"),
    "GPT-3": (7, -2, "left"),
    "GPT-4": (-6, -3, "right"),
    "frontier (est.)": (-6, 4, "right"),
}
for x, y, lab, show in points:
    if show:
        dx, dy, ha = offsets.get(lab, (5, -2, "left"))
        ax.annotate(lab, (x, y), textcoords="offset points", xytext=(dx, dy), fontsize=5.7, color=COLORS["ink"], ha=ha)

# Overlay a representative silicon design cycle band (e.g., 2021-2024)
ax.axvspan(2021, 2024.5, color=COLORS["purple"], alpha=0.1, zorder=1)
ax.text(2022.75, 1e11, "typical silicon\ndesign cadence\n(3-4 years)", ha="center", va="center", fontsize=5.5, color=COLORS["designspace_ink"])

ax.grid(axis="y", color=COLORS["grid"], linewidth=0.6)
for spine in ["top", "right"]:
    ax.spines[spine].set_visible(False)
ax.spines["left"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
ax.spines["bottom"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
```

This growing need to track moving workloads reflects a broader breakdown of abstractions, a shift that Compiler 2.0 offers as a useful adjacent warning. Amarasinghe's framing is that
compilers originally made hardware disappear for programmers, but multicore
processors, vector instructions, accelerators, and heterogeneous systems have
pushed more performance burden back onto programmers
[@Amarasinghe2020Compiler20; @Amarasinghe2026Compiler20CSAIL]. The same
pattern appears at the architecture level. Abstractions still matter, but the
design loop must now expose more of the workload, software, hardware, and
physical state that earlier abstractions could hide.

To manage this rapidly changing software state, the community has had to formalize its workload agreements. MLPerf (introduced in @sec-moonshot) is the clearest example
[@MattsonEtAl2020MLPerf]. MLPerf Inference sharpened the deployment-facing
version of that problem. The paper reports more than 100 organizations building
ML inference chips, systems spanning at least three orders of magnitude in
power and five orders of magnitude in performance, and more than 600
reproducible measurements from 14 organizations in the first submission round
[@ReddiEtAl2020MLPerfInference]. The lesson is not only that benchmarks
need rules. It is that a benchmark must encode scenarios, latency constraints,
accuracy targets, software stacks, and comparability rules before performance
numbers mean the same thing across systems
[@ReddiEtAl2021VisionBehindMLPerf]. That is also the challenge for
architecture. A benchmark is not a fixed oracle. It is a maintained agreement
about what evidence should count for a class of systems.[^fn-inst-nist] When
that agreement changes, an earlier workload comparison may no longer answer the
same question.

[^fn-inst-nist]: Metrology institutions embody the same discipline. A national standards body such as the U.S. National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) exists because a measurement supports engineering and commerce only while someone maintains the reference standards and calibration chain that keep a stated unit meaning the same thing everywhere. A maintained benchmark is architecture's reference standard, and like any reference standard it decays without that custodial work.

For the lighthouse prompt, the workload is not merely "XR." It is a moving
bundle of sensing, perception, graphics, display, interaction, latency,
quality-of-experience, and energy constraints. Even with XRBench
[@KwonEtAl2023XRBench], a credible architecture loop must still decide
which traces, model versions, deadlines, input distributions, and quality
targets matter. If the software stack changes faster than the hardware loop
can absorb, the design may optimize yesterday's workload.

## Physical Constraints Move Into Architecture {#sec-physical-constraints-move-into-architecture}

Feedback-bound pressure appears when the physical measurement that can overturn
an architectural ranking is costly or arrives late. Representation pressure
appears when the early model omits that physical effect. Architecture does not
sit above physical reality. It is the layer where software intent, hardware
mechanisms, and physical constraints meet, without replacing the specialist EDA
and implementation flows that verify them.

For example, data-movement estimates can reverse an early compute-centered
ranking rather than merely confirm that memory is expensive. Moving data through the memory hierarchy often
costs far more energy than arithmetic, and Horowitz's widely used energy
estimates made this point concrete for a generation of architects
[@Horowitz2014Energy]. That changes what architecture work means. A design loop
cannot only ask which compute block is fastest. It must
ask where the data lives, how often it moves, who schedules it, what locality
exists, what precision is acceptable, and what the software stack can express.

A useful architecture-level decomposition is
$$
E_{\mathrm{system}} =
E_{\mathrm{compute}} + E_{\mathrm{memory}} +
E_{\mathrm{interconnect}} + E_{\mathrm{control}} +
E_{\mathrm{leakage}} .
$$
This is not a circuit-level energy model. It is a diagnostic check on whether
the comparison represents every major source of energy that could change the
decision. A candidate that
reduces arithmetic but increases memory movement, interconnect traffic, control
overhead, or leakage has not necessarily improved the system. Control engineers call the frequency-domain version of this pattern the *waterbed effect* [@Bode1945NetworkAnalysis]. Here the analogy is qualitative. No conservation law forces memory energy up when compute energy falls, but an optimization that watches only one term leaves the others free to rise (@fig-waterbed-effect).

![**The waterbed effect in the energy decomposition:** An optimization that watches only one term leaves the others free to rise. A naive AI optimization that fails to represent data movement squeezes the compute segment while the memory and interconnect segments balloon, pushing the stack past the dashed line that marks the baseline total. Bar heights are illustrative, not measured energy breakdowns.](images/F2-waterbed-effect){#fig-waterbed-effect width="100%" fig-alt="Bar chart showing a baseline energy breakdown alongside a naive AI-optimized candidate where compute energy shrinks but memory and interconnect energy balloon."}

Interconnect costs have the same character as memory movement. On-chip networks, package links, memory
interfaces, collectives, and host-device protocols define what a design can
actually sustain. EDA and physical-design constraints also move upward. Timing,
placement, routing, IR drop, thermal behavior, power delivery networks (PDN), leakage, signoff, and test are
not late implementation details when they can overturn an architectural choice. Power and thermal density, for instance, can flag a dense accelerator mapping as risky early, and IR-drop and thermal signoff confirm or clear it later, once a placed netlist and current profile exist. Because physical-design tools use heuristic optimization, one failed closure attempt does not identify whether the candidate or the tool setup caused the failure. Constraints, seeds, and repeated outcomes are needed to tell those cases apart.

This is the diagnostic distinction. Missing physical assumptions indicate a
representation bound. Long waits for the measurement that settles the question
indicate a feedback bound. Candidates that keep advancing despite contrary
measurements indicate a rejection bound. The later chapters define how to
represent and act on those distinctions.

The order-of-magnitude spread in @fig-data-movement-energy-scale is not
something to memorize or treat as a current-node prediction. The architectural
use is simpler. Local arithmetic and memory movement live on very different
energy scales, so a loop that optimizes only arithmetic can improve the wrong
thing. Advanced-node designs do not remove this lesson; if anything, the gap
between local logic and moving data, driving wires, and feeding memory systems
is one reason locality remains an architectural problem rather than a solved
device-scaling detail. For the 3\ nm-class lighthouse prompt, the loop would
need a fresh power model before making a design decision.

```{python}
#| label: fig-data-movement-energy-scale
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **Data movement can dominate arithmetic energy:** Rough Horowitz 45\ nm energy values show why architecture loops must represent locality, buffering, precision, scheduling, and memory hierarchy rather than counting arithmetic alone. The values are order-of-magnitude interpretive anchors, not current-node device estimates.
#| out-width: "92%"
#| fig-alt: "Log-scale plot comparing arithmetic and memory-access energy, showing that SRAM and DRAM movement cost orders of magnitude more than local arithmetic."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, add_note_box, apply_style, draw_range_rows, row_axis, top_log_axis

rows = [
    {"display_label": "32b integer add", "display_note": "baseline arithmetic", "energy_low_pj": 0.1, "energy_high_pj": 0.1, "right_label": "1$\\times$", "color": COLORS["blue"]},
    {"display_label": "32b floating-point add", "display_note": "local arithmetic", "energy_low_pj": 0.9, "energy_high_pj": 0.9, "right_label": "9$\\times$", "color": COLORS["blue"]},
    {"display_label": "32b integer multiply", "display_note": "local arithmetic", "energy_low_pj": 3.1, "energy_high_pj": 3.1, "right_label": "31$\\times$", "color": COLORS["blue"]},
    {"display_label": "32b floating-point multiply", "display_note": "local arithmetic", "energy_low_pj": 3.7, "energy_high_pj": 3.7, "right_label": "37$\\times$", "color": COLORS["blue"]},
    {"display_label": "8 KB SRAM access", "display_note": "64b cache access", "energy_low_pj": 10, "energy_high_pj": 10, "right_label": "100$\\times$", "color": COLORS["green"]},
    {"display_label": "32 KB SRAM access", "display_note": "64b cache access", "energy_low_pj": 20, "energy_high_pj": 20, "right_label": "200$\\times$", "color": COLORS["green"]},
    {"display_label": "1 MB SRAM access", "display_note": "on-chip memory access", "energy_low_pj": 100, "energy_high_pj": 100, "right_label": "1000$\\times$", "color": COLORS["orange"]},
    {"display_label": "off-chip DRAM access", "display_note": "off-chip memory access", "energy_low_pj": 1300, "energy_high_pj": 1300, "right_label": "~13000$\\times$", "color": COLORS["red"]},
]

apply_style()
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4.9, 3.45))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.40, right=0.80, top=0.82, bottom=0.17)

row_axis(ax, len(rows))
top_log_axis(
    ax,
    xlim=(0.06, 4000),
    xticks=[0.1, 1, 10, 100, 1000],
    xticklabels=["0.1 pJ", "1 pJ", "10 pJ", "100 pJ", "1 nJ"],
    xlabel="rough energy per operation or access",
)
draw_range_rows(ax, rows, low_key="energy_low_pj", high_key="energy_high_pj", label_x=-0.82, right_x=1.04, label_fontsize=6.6, note_fontsize=5.4, right_fontsize=6.1, label_dy=-0.17, note_dy=0.23)
add_note_box(fig, "Rough 45 nm, 0.9 V values from Horowitz; use for orders of magnitude.", xywh=(0.14, 0.035, 0.72, 0.075), fontsize=5.5)

plt.show()
plt.close(fig)
```

The plot changes the section's claim from "data movement is expensive" to
"locality is an early rejection condition". A loop that cannot represent memory
movement cannot support a power or efficiency commitment.

## Engineering Cost Makes Feedback Scarce

Feedback-bound pressure is ultimately an engineering-capacity problem. The
scissors gap in @fig-scissors-gap compares the growing burden of choices,
constraints, software paths, and physical feasibility with the capacity to
measure and review them. The first can grow without a matching increase in the
second.

![**The scissors gap is a feedback gap:** Architecture pressure rises when design choices, constraints, and evidence demand grow faster than unaided manual coordination and verification capacity. The widening region is the gap that Architecture 2.0 tries to make explicit and manageable; @fig-verification-demand-scissors quantifies the same widening with measured staffing demand.](images/F2-scissors-gap){#fig-scissors-gap width="92%" fig-alt="Scissors-style diagram where rising choices, constraints, and evidence demand separate from slower manual verification capacity, creating a feedback gap."}

As a silicon-facing scale anchor, the gap is also an engineering-cost problem.
Public estimates vary, and they should not be treated as universal accounting
rules, but their scale is useful.
The Semiconductor Industry Association reports that the cost of designing a
latest-node chip rose from about \$30M for a 65\ nm chip in 2006 to more than
\$540M for a 5\ nm chip in 2020, a greater than $18\times$ increase
[@SemiconductorIndustryAssociation2026ChipDesign]. A McKinsey analysis
gives a similar order of magnitude, estimating roughly \$175M for a 10\ nm
design, \$300M for a 7\ nm design, and \$540M for a 5\ nm design when validation,
IP qualification, and related development costs are included
[@BauerEtAl2020SemiconductorDesignManufacturing]. These are not only mask
or wafer costs. They are costs of architecture, design, validation,
verification, IP, tools, and people.

Verification makes the people cost visible. In a summary of the 2022 Wilson
Research Group functional-verification study, Foster reports that demand for
IC/ASIC verification engineers grew nearly three times as fast as demand for
design engineers from 2007 to 2022 (@fig-verification-demand-scissors); the same summary reports that mean peak staffing is roughly one verification
engineer per design engineer across most market segments, that processor
projects can reach a 5-to-1 verification-to-design ratio, and that
design engineers spent 49 percent of their time in verification in 2022
[@Foster2022WilsonVerificationStudy]. A later 2024 Wilson Research Group
IC/ASIC report makes the commitment risk visible from another angle. It
reports first-silicon success at 14 percent, the lowest level in two decades
[@Foster2025WilsonVerification2024]. Each invalid candidate consumes scarce
engineering capacity and adds commitment risk, not just compute cycles. Generated
candidates are cheap only until they consume verification, tool, and review
capacity.

```{python}
#| label: fig-verification-demand-scissors
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **The scissors gap in measured head-count:** Mean peak demand for IC/ASIC verification engineers grew about 146 percent from 2007 to 2022 while demand for design engineers grew about 50 percent, a 6.2 versus 2.7 percent compound annual growth rate [@Foster2022WilsonVerificationStudy]. These are the measured blades of the conceptual gap in @fig-scissors-gap. Filled markers carry printed data labels in the published study figures; hollow 2022 markers are chart-read approximations constrained by the study's stated growth figures.
#| out-width: "78%"
#| fig-alt: "Two-line chart indexing engineer demand to 2007, with verification-engineer demand rising to about 2.5 times its 2007 level by 2022 while design-engineer demand reaches about 1.5 times, opening a widening gap between the lines."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, apply_style

apply_style()

# Mean peak engineers per IC/ASIC project, Wilson Research Group / Siemens EDA
# functional-verification studies (Foster's published summaries). 2007-2020
# values carry printed data labels; 2022 values are chart-read, constrained by
# the study's stated callouts of +50% design and +146% verification demand
# growth since 2007 (2.7% vs 6.2% CAGR).
# Receipt: data/source-receipts/chapter2-wilson-verification-staffing.csv
years = [2007, 2012, 2016, 2020, 2022]
design = [7.8, 8.5, 10.5, 10.3, 11.7]
verif = [4.8, 8.4, 11.6, 11.4, 11.8]

design_idx = [v / design[0] for v in design]
verif_idx = [v / verif[0] for v in verif]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4.65, 2.3))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.12, right=0.80, top=0.93, bottom=0.19)

ax.plot(years, verif_idx, color=COLORS["green"], linewidth=1.6, zorder=2)
ax.plot(years, design_idx, color=COLORS["blue"], linewidth=1.6, zorder=2)
ax.scatter(years[:-1], verif_idx[:-1], s=22, color=COLORS["green"], zorder=3)
ax.scatter(years[:-1], design_idx[:-1], s=20, marker="s", color=COLORS["blue"], zorder=3)
# 2022 values are chart-read approximations: draw them hollow
ax.scatter([years[-1]], [verif_idx[-1]], s=26, facecolors="white",
           edgecolors=COLORS["green"], linewidths=1.1, zorder=3)
ax.scatter([years[-1]], [design_idx[-1]], s=24, marker="s", facecolors="white",
           edgecolors=COLORS["blue"], linewidths=1.1, zorder=3)

ax.text(2022.6, verif_idx[-1], "verification\nengineers (+146%)", va="center",
        fontsize=6.0, color=COLORS["green"], clip_on=False)
ax.text(2022.6, design_idx[-1], "design\nengineers (+50%)", va="center",
        fontsize=6.0, color=COLORS["blue"], clip_on=False)

ax.set_xlim(2006, 2023)
ax.set_ylim(0.9, 2.7)
ax.set_xticks([2007, 2012, 2016, 2020, 2022])
ax.set_yticks([1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5])
ax.set_yticklabels(["1.0$\\times$", "1.5$\\times$", "2.0$\\times$", "2.5$\\times$"])
ax.set_ylabel("mean peak demand vs. 2007", fontsize=7)
ax.tick_params(axis="both", labelsize=6.5, length=2.5, width=0.6, pad=2)
ax.grid(axis="y", color=COLORS["grid"], linewidth=0.6)

for spine in ["top", "right"]:
    ax.spines[spine].set_visible(False)
ax.spines["left"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
ax.spines["bottom"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])

plt.show()
plt.close(fig)
```

::: {.callout-field-note title="The bug five entries wide: Pentium FDIV"}
The 1994 Pentium FDIV bug cost Intel roughly \$475M because five lookup-table
entries were wrong, and no cheap check caught it before silicon
[@IntelPentiumFDIV].

**Lesson:** The episode shows why a missed early check can turn a small defect into a large commitment cost.
:::

The public dollar estimates become a scale check in
@fig-leading-node-design-cost. The robust point is simpler. As the loop moves
toward leading-edge implementation, feedback and commitment consume real
engineering budgets, not only simulator cycles.

```{python}
#| label: fig-leading-node-design-cost
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **Leading-node design-cost estimates make the scissors concrete:** Public SIA and McKinsey estimates [@SemiconductorIndustryAssociation2026ChipDesign; @BauerEtAl2020SemiconductorDesignManufacturing] put latest-node chip design costs from tens of millions of dollars at 65 nm to more than $500M at 5 nm, with IBS estimates extending the trend to roughly $725M at 2 nm [@InternationalBusinessStrategies2024ChipDesignCost]. The accounting boundaries vary, but the scale makes feedback, validation, IP, tools, and human engineering effort part of the architecture loop.
#| out-width: "86%"
#| fig-alt: "Line and area plot showing public chip design cost estimates increasing from tens of millions at 65 nanometers to roughly 725 million dollars at 2 nanometers."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, add_note_box, apply_style

rows = [
    {"node_label": "65 nm", "cost_musd": 30, "source": "SIA", "right_label": "~$30M"},
    {"node_label": "10 nm", "cost_musd": 175, "source": "McKinsey", "right_label": "~$175M"},
    {"node_label": "7 nm", "cost_musd": 300, "source": "McKinsey", "right_label": "~$300M"},
    {"node_label": "5 nm", "cost_musd": 540, "source": "SIA / McKinsey", "right_label": ">$540M"},
    {"node_label": "3 nm", "cost_musd": 590, "source": "IBS", "right_label": "~$590M"},
    {"node_label": "2 nm", "cost_musd": 725, "source": "IBS", "right_label": "~$725M"},
]

apply_style()
labels = [row["node_label"] for row in rows]
costs = [row["cost_musd"] for row in rows]
sources = [row["source"] for row in rows]
right_labels = [row["right_label"] for row in rows]
x_values = list(range(len(rows)))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4.65, 2.25))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.14, right=0.95, top=0.88, bottom=0.33)

ax.plot(x_values, costs, color=COLORS["blue"], linewidth=1.7, marker="s", markersize=4.5, markerfacecolor=COLORS["note_fill"], markeredgewidth=1.0)
ax.fill_between(x_values, costs, [0] * len(costs), color=COLORS["design_cost_fill"], alpha=0.75, zorder=0)
ax.set_xlim(-0.35, len(rows) - 0.65)
ax.set_ylim(0, 800)
ax.set_xticks(x_values)
ax.set_xticklabels(labels, fontsize=6.8)
ax.set_yticks([0, 100, 300, 500, 700])
ax.set_yticklabels(["$0", "$100M", "$300M", "$500M", "$700M"], fontsize=6.5)
ax.set_ylabel("public design-cost estimate", fontsize=7)
ax.tick_params(axis="both", length=2.5, width=0.6, pad=2)
ax.grid(axis="y", color=COLORS["grid"], linewidth=0.6)

for spine in ["top", "right"]:
    ax.spines[spine].set_visible(False)
ax.spines["left"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
ax.spines["bottom"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])

for x, cost, source, right_label in zip(x_values, costs, sources, right_labels):
    ax.text(x, cost + 24, right_label, ha="center", va="bottom", fontsize=6.6, fontweight="bold", color=COLORS["ink"])
    ax.text(x, -83, source, ha="center", va="top", fontsize=5.7, color=COLORS["muted"], clip_on=False)

add_note_box(fig, "Directional estimates: accounting boundaries vary by chip, node, IP reuse, and organization.", xywh=(0.16, 0.04, 0.72, 0.12), fontsize=5.8)

plt.show()
plt.close(fig)
```

The cost plot is not an indictment of architects. It shows why feedback can be
the scarce resource even when candidate generation is cheap. If the relevant
tools, verification effort, or review time cannot keep up, the loop is
feedback-bound before added proposals enter it.

## Rejection Cannot Keep Up With Proposals {#sec-feedback-and-verification-become-the-bottleneck}

Feedback-bound pressure becomes rejection-bound when comparable proposals
arrive faster than the available checks and reviewers can settle them. The
result is a queue, not merely uncertainty about one candidate. That queue drains through progressively costlier checks, from cheap surrogate
screening through simulation to accountable architecture review, and the
illustrative funnel in @fig-rejection-bottleneck narrows at each stage until
human review settles only a handful of candidates a
day. @sec-loop-patterns-across-stack later
states the routing and cost assumptions needed for a precise capacity model.

![**An Illustrative Rejection Bottleneck:** Once proposals exceed trusted disposition capacity, more candidates grow the queue. This funnel shows one possible adjudication path in which accountable architecture review is the final capacity limit; its candidate counts are illustrative, not a measured throughput law.](images/F2-rejection-bottleneck){#fig-rejection-bottleneck width="100%" fig-alt="Illustrative funnel showing AI-generated candidates narrowing through surrogate screening and simulation to an accountable architecture-review stage labeled as five candidates per day."}

The verification economics of the previous section put a bound on this
disposition path. Because those staffing ratios make trusted
disposition capacity a head-count, not a compute budget, what slips past
that head-count surfaces at the most expensive gate. Across the same study
series, first-silicon success has fallen from roughly a third of IC/ASIC
projects to the 14 percent low noted above (@fig-first-silicon-trend). A
saturated rejection path looks, from the commitment side, like a falling
first-silicon success rate.

Architecture measurements differ in cost and in what they can settle. A
spreadsheet model is cheap but limited. Simulation may be more informative but
slow or biased. Synthesis and physical design expose implementation effects but
depend on settings and constraints. Silicon and deployment telemetry arrive
after major commitments. @sec-feedback-verification-trust explains how those
observations become evidence. The present diagnosis asks only whether the
disposition path can keep up with admitted proposals.

```{python}
#| label: fig-first-silicon-trend
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **First-silicon success keeps falling:** The share of IC/ASIC projects achieving first-silicon success fell from roughly one third, near the two-decade norm, to 14 percent in 2024, the lowest level the study series has recorded [@Foster2022WilsonVerificationStudy; @Foster2025WilsonVerification2024]. Filled markers are values the reports state directly; hollow markers are chart-read approximations from published study figures. Each spin that fails is a candidate the loop could not reject before its most expensive gate.
#| out-width: "78%"
#| fig-alt: "Line chart of first-silicon success rates for IC/ASIC projects from 2012 to 2024, falling from about a third of projects to 14 percent in 2024, below a dashed 30 percent two-decade reference line."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, apply_style

apply_style()

# First-silicon success rate (% of IC/ASIC projects), Wilson Research Group /
# Siemens EDA functional-verification studies (Foster's published summaries).
# 2020, 2022, and 2024 values are stated in the reports; 2012-2018 values are
# chart-read approximations from unlabeled study figures; ~30% is the stated
# two-decade historical norm.
# Receipt: data/source-receipts/chapter2-wilson-first-silicon-success.csv
chart_read = [(2012, 33.5), (2014, 31), (2016, 33), (2018, 26)]
stated = [(2020, 32), (2022, 24), (2024, 14)]
series = sorted(chart_read + stated)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4.65, 2.3))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.13, right=0.95, top=0.93, bottom=0.19)

ax.axhline(30, color=COLORS["muted"], linewidth=0.9, linestyle=(0, (4, 3)), zorder=1)
ax.text(2024.8, 31.0, "~30% two-decade historical norm", fontsize=5.8,
        color=COLORS["muted"], ha="right", va="bottom")

ax.plot([p[0] for p in series], [p[1] for p in series], color=COLORS["red"],
        linewidth=1.6, zorder=2)
ax.scatter([p[0] for p in stated], [p[1] for p in stated], s=24,
           color=COLORS["red"], zorder=3)
ax.scatter([p[0] for p in chart_read], [p[1] for p in chart_read], s=24,
           facecolors="white", edgecolors=COLORS["red"], linewidths=1.1, zorder=3)
ax.annotate("14%", (2024, 14), textcoords="offset points", xytext=(-2, -11),
            fontsize=6.4, fontweight="bold", color=COLORS["red"])

ax.set_xlim(2011, 2025)
ax.set_ylim(0, 42)
ax.set_xticks([2012, 2016, 2020, 2024])
ax.set_yticks([0, 10, 20, 30, 40])
ax.set_yticklabels(["0%", "10%", "20%", "30%", "40%"])
ax.set_ylabel("first-silicon success", fontsize=7)
ax.tick_params(axis="both", labelsize=6.5, length=2.5, width=0.6, pad=2)
ax.grid(axis="y", color=COLORS["grid"], linewidth=0.6)

for spine in ["top", "right"]:
    ax.spines[spine].set_visible(False)
ax.spines["left"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
ax.spines["bottom"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])

plt.show()
plt.close(fig)
```

::: {#pri-treat-feedback .callout-design-principle title="Diagnose the Loop Before Adding AI"}
Determine whether representation, action, feedback, rejection, or commitment
cannot keep up. Add AI only where it relieves that bound without silently moving
the pressure to another part of the loop.
:::

## Architecture Violates Generic AI Assumptions

A conventional machine-learning workflow is still useful as a foil. Students
often learn a pipeline that runs from data collection to preprocessing,
training, validation, deployment, and monitoring. That familiar picture still
holds, but a bounded architecture study cannot be a simple pipeline, as
@fig-ai-workflow-mismatch shows. Its execution path must carry represented state, legal actions, costly
feedback, provenance, workload drift, rejected candidates, and the
architectural claim an accountable decision role reviews. The checklist
version of this mismatch appears in
@tbl-ai-assumptions-architecture-violations; each row pairs an assumption a
generic pipeline can safely make with the architecture reality that breaks it
and what the loop must do in response.

![**A bounded architecture study needs more than a generic ML pipeline:** A generic ML workflow can be taught as data, preprocessing, training, validation, and deployment. An architecture study must also expose represented state, legal actions, costly feedback, rejected candidates, workload drift, an architectural claim, and the role accountable for the decision.](images/F2c-ai-workflow-mismatch){#fig-ai-workflow-mismatch width="100%" fig-alt="Comparison diagram showing a generic machine-learning pipeline alongside a bounded architecture study with represented state, legal actions, costly feedback, rejected candidates, drift, claim review, and an accountable decision role."}

The five-part diagnostic is necessary because architecture is not a standard
machine-learning problem. Introductory supervised-learning pipelines and many benchmarked AI tasks assume
abundant data, cheap feedback, stable labels, and clear losses. Those are exactly the conditions a design loop would
want, and computer architecture violates them at almost every boundary. Data are often proprietary, incomplete,
stale, or missing the rejected and failed candidates that explain why a design
space has certain boundaries. Feedback ranges
from a quick proxy to a simulation, synthesis run, physical-design report,
expert review, emulation result, or silicon measurement, each with different
latency, cost, fidelity, and authority. The action space is also unusual. Many
generated configurations are not merely low quality; they are illegal,
unsupported by tools, unverifiable, or incompatible with software and physical
constraints.

The contrast is visible in familiar community benchmarks. SPEC CPU and MLPerf
do not only provide inputs. They provide run rules, reporting conventions,
versioned tasks, and a basis for comparing claims [@SPEC2017CPU;
@MattsonEtAl2020MLPerf]. Architecture 2.0 needs comparable discipline across the
state, actions, measurements, dispositions, and decisions that produce a score.

The result is not a simple lack of data. It is a mismatch between generic AI
assumptions and architecture-loop requirements. The diagnostic reveals where
that mismatch enters a particular project before the later chapters prescribe
the representation, environment, method, and review machinery.

| **Common AI assumption** | **Architecture violation** | **Loop implication** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Abundant labeled data | Many labels are proprietary, expensive, stale, or not recorded. | Check whether the available state covers the decision. |
| Cheap feedback | Simulation, synthesis, EDA, emulation, and review can be slow or scarce. | Check whether measurement capacity matches decision demand. |
| Stable distribution | Workloads, software stacks, compilers, models, and deployment policies drift. | Revisit decisions when their workload assumptions change. |
| Valid actions are easy to define | Many generated configurations are invalid, unsafe, unverifiable, or unsupported by tools. | Separate legal choices from invalid or unsupported moves. |
| Reward is clear | Efficiency mixes performance, energy, area, cost, reliability, sustainability, verification burden, and risk. | State which tradeoffs the decision is allowed to make. |
| Proxy metrics are enough | Proxy wins can vanish under stronger checks or different workloads. | Record which conclusions a proxy cannot settle. |
| Failures are just bad samples | Failed runs, rejected candidates, and invalid states describe the design space. | Preserve why alternatives failed or were rejected. |

: **Generic AI assumptions break at architecture boundaries:** Architecture loops must represent proprietary or incomplete data, expensive feedback, invalid actions, drifting workloads, multiobjective efficiency, proxy failures, and rejected or failed candidates. {#tbl-ai-assumptions-architecture-violations tbl-colwidths="[28,31,30]"}

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

This mismatch does not make AI irrelevant. It makes loop diagnosis a
prerequisite for deciding where AI can help. AI can summarize tool outputs,
propose candidates, search spaces, predict costs, construct tests, critique
assumptions, retrieve prior designs, and coordinate subtasks, but whether any
capability helps depends on the diagnosed bound. A faster generator does not
repair missing workload state. A predictor does not widen an action space that
excludes the needed design. A ranking does not create measurement or review
capacity. @sec-architecture-20-ontology supplies the minimum review object
needed to state that distinction for a concrete claim.

## Open Research Questions

The strain on classical design loops makes the AI question narrower than
generic automation. The first research problem is to know when AI assistance is
warranted, where it should enter the system, and what pressure signals should
force stronger evidence before expensive validation or human commitment.

1. **What measurements reveal a strained architecture loop?**
A loop becomes strained when candidate volume, feedback cost, validation
burden, software drift, and human review latency grow faster than the team's
ability to reject and revise. A useful profile would predict wasted
high-fidelity runs, missed invalid candidates, delayed decisions, or stale
evidence. Useful measurements include invalid-action rates, feedback latency
and cost, verification staffing, high-fidelity sample budgets, and decision
turnaround. The profile should explain the scissors gap in @fig-scissors-gap
and the cost pressure in @fig-leading-node-design-cost better than candidate
count alone.

2. **Where should AI enter specialized and chiplet design loops?**
Specialization and chiplets expand the loop across workload selection,
software paths, microarchitecture, integration, packaging, validation, and
physical feasibility. AI should enter where the bottleneck is measurable, not
where a model can produce more candidates. A strong study would compare entry
points under the same validation budget and report invalid candidates avoided,
missed software obligations, high-fidelity checks saved, and lost tradeoff
quality. The expanding design spaces in @tbl-design-loop-scale-anchors and the
diagnostic in @tbl-loop-bottleneck-diagnostic provide concrete
testbeds.

3. **Which physical signals should triage expensive validation?**
Physical reality becomes an architecture concern when data movement, timing
margin, congestion, power density, thermal behavior, or closure risk can
overturn an early architectural win. A useful triage suite would compare early
signals against later synthesis, floorplanning, placement, routing, static
timing analysis, design-rule checking, layout-versus-schematic checking, electromigration and voltage drop (EM/IR),
and power outcomes. The evaluation should report false accepts, false rejects,
inconclusive runs, and engineering effort saved. The goal is earlier routing
toward rejection, revision, or stronger evidence, not signoff-quality
prediction.

4. **When does workload drift expire architecture evidence?**
The software and workload pressure described in
@sec-software-changes-faster-than-silicon means architecture evidence can become
stale before a decision reaches deployment. A research contribution would attach
validity horizons to claims, using workload versions, input-distribution
summaries, compiler or runtime assumptions, field telemetry, and re-evaluation
triggers. The evaluation could replay historical workload changes or fleet
signals and ask when a claim should be maintained, weakened, or withdrawn. This
turns workload characterization into part of the evidence record, not a setup
detail.

5. **When do multiobjective tradeoffs overwhelm manual evaluation?**
Modern efficiency combines performance, energy, power delivery, reliability,
sustainability, cost, and time to evidence. Manual review breaks down when the
Pareto surface changes faster than the team can inspect tradeoffs at the needed
fidelity. A strong paper would define pressure metrics for objective count,
constraint coupling, feedback cost, and reviewer latency, then show when a
bounded AI role improves triage without authorizing unsupported commitments.
This keeps the question centered on where AI should help, not on using AI
because it is available.

## Conclusion

This chapter asked which part of an architecture loop cannot keep up before AI
is added. The pressure is structural, not a matter of taste. Specialization,
heterogeneous integration, data movement, software velocity, physical
constraints, and validation burden can strain the state a loop represents, the
choices it exposes, the measurements it can afford, the candidates it can
settle, or the decisions it can justify.

The five diagnoses prevent one bottleneck from standing in for all of them. A
loop missing decisive workload state is not repaired by faster feedback. A loop
with the wrong action space is not repaired by more candidates. A loop with
scarce measurements needs a different intervention from one whose review queue
is saturated or whose evidence does not authorize a clear next decision.

AI assistance earns its place when it relieves the diagnosed bound without
silently moving pressure elsewhere. The bounded study must preserve enough
state, failures, evidence, rejection paths, and decision authority for another
architect to see whether that happened. The goal is not a larger search space.
It is a reviewable study in which additional automation produces more supported
findings and decisions rather than more unresolved work.

::: {.callout-carry-forward}
- **Carry forward:** Diagnose whether representation, action, feedback,
  rejection, or commitment cannot keep up before deciding where AI belongs.
- **Reader test:** Can you identify the first bound in a strained architecture
  loop and explain why relieving a different one would only move the pressure?
- **Up next:** @sec-architecture-20-ontology makes a bounded architecture study
  reviewable by distinguishing its scope, artifacts, architectural claim,
  execution loop, evidence, and decision boundary.
:::
